DISCOVERY 301, DEFENDANT ALEJANDRA GARCIA'S MOTION TO COMPEL OBJECTION-FREE RESPONSES TO HER FIRST SET OF REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS IS GRANTED. HARRINGTON IS ORDERED TO PROVIDE COMPLETE, VERIFIED, OBJECTION-FREE RESPONSES TO THE FIRST SET OF REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION BY MARCH 30, 2026. GARCIA'S REQUEST FOR JUDICIAL NOTICE IS DENIED. THE DOCUMENTS FOR WHICH COUNSEL SEEKS JUDICIAL NOTICE ARE NOT RELEVANT TO THIS MOTION.HARRINGTON IS ORDERED TO PROVIDE COMPLETE, VERIFIED, OBJECTION-FREE RESPONSES TO THE FIRST SET OF REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION BY MARCH 30, 2026. GARCIA'S REQUEST FOR JUDICIAL NOTICE IS DENIED. GARCIA SEEKS SANCTIONS. THE COURT ORDERS HARRINGTON TO PAY $1500 TO GARCIA BY APRIL 10, 2026. (SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING) PROPOSED ORDER SUBMITTED. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN; CLERK: W. TRUPEK; NOT REPORTED (301/CVA)

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce The Court'S March 9, 2026, Order And For Monetary Sanctions As To Plaintiff Linda Harrington And Her Attorney Of Record, Dylan Hackett
Off Calendar Apr-24-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT ALEJANDRA GARCIA'S MOTION TO ENFORCE THE COURT'S MARCH 9, 2026, ORDER AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS AS TO PLAINTIFF LINDA HARRINGTON AND HER ATTORNEY OF RECORD, DYLAN HACKETT

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Alejandra Garcia's motion to enforce the March 9, 2026 order and for monetary sanctions is denied. Plaintiff Linda Harrington's request for sanctions is denied. This motion, much less the reply papers, should never have been filed. The opposition papers should have been a single page. It appears that the motion arises out of the distaste that counsel have for one another and their desire to litigate that distaste in court. While the court cannot require that counsel like each other, the court can and does require adherence to the rules of civility. The rush to file this motion should not have been necessary. The request for additional sanctions is punitive and contrary to the spirit of the discovery statutes. The court does not know whether Harrington's counsel would have paid the sanctions order less quickly but for this motion, but that certainly seems possible based on the papers submitted. Due to the shortness of life and the desire to avoid further motions such as this one, neither side is awarded sanctions on this motion. Counsel on both sides should be able to find ways to spend their time more productively than filing and opposing this motion. Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/HEK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Request For Production, To Compel Production, To Deem All Objections Waived, And For Monetary Sanctions From Plaintiff Linda Harrington And Plaintiffs Counsel Of Record
Off Calendar Mar-10-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference

Danielle Gilliland, Esq., Law Offices, 520-440-6221 daniellegilliland@gmail.com, Specially appearing on behalf of Dylan Hacket, Counsel for Plaintiff

Alejandra Garcia is present in court, but not accompanied by her counsel of record Jeffrey Bruce Neustadt

Matter is contested late by Plaintiff's counsel. Court does not allow argument due to the matter not being properly contested. Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ALEJANDRA GARCIA'S Motion To Compel Responses To Request For Production, To Compel Production, To Deem All Objections Waived, And For Monetary Sanctions From Plaintiff Linda Harrington And Plaintiffs Counsel Of Record.

Defendant Alejandra Garcia's motion to compel objection-free responses to her first set of requests for production of documents is granted. Plaintiff Linda Harrington did not timely respond, has thereby waived objections, did not seek a protective order, and makes no showing that she has provided substantially compliant discovery responses that would enable her, upon a noticed motion, to obtain relief from waiver. Although counsel contends a medical emergency prevented Harrington's response, Harrington provides no competent, nonhearsay evidence of a medical emergency and did not seek a protective order. The evidence before the court casts doubt on the claim of medical emergency.

Harrington is ordered to provide complete, verified, objection-free responses to the first set of requests for production by March 30, 2026.

Garcia's request for judicial notice is denied. The documents for which counsel seeks judicial notice are not relevant to this motion.

Garcia seeks sanctions. The court "shall" award sanctions when a party moves to compel responses to unanswered discovery unless it finds substantial justification or unjust circumstances. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 2031.300, subd. (c).) The court does not find unjust circumstances or substantial justification but reduces the sanctions award. A motion to compel responses to unanswered discovery requires a simple showing; Garcia's motion included extraneous material. The court orders Harrington to pay $1500 to Garcia by April 10, 2026.

Proposed order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

Motion To Compel Responses To Form Interrogatories, To Deem All Objections Waived, And For Monetary Sanctions From Plaintiff Linda Harrington And Plaintiffs Counsel Of Record
Off Calendar Mar-06-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT ALEJANDRA GARCIA'S MOTION TO COMPEL RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES, TO DEEM ALL OBJECTIONS WAIVED, AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS FROM PLAINTIFF LINDA HARRINGTON AND PLAINTIFFS' COUNSEL OF RECORD.

Appearances:
Jeffrey Neustadt of the Law Offices of Jeffrey B. Neustadt (415.434.4440, jbneustadtlaw@gmail.com);
Chris Quattrociocche of the Hackett Law Firm (415.410.9931) for plaintiffs and responding parties.

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Alejandra Garcia's motion to compel responses to form interrogatories and for monetary sanctions is granted in part. No later than March 5, 2026 plaintiff Linda Harrington must serve verified code-complaint responses to the form interrogatories served on her and the only objections she may assert are privilege and work product. No later than March 19, 2026 plaintiff must pay $1,000 in reasonable monetary sanctions to Garcia. In the exercise of its discretion, the court will not entertain live evidence on this motion. Even if plaintiff was not able to respond to the interrogatories on a timely basis, it was her obligation to notify Garcia on a timely basis that she was unable to do so, which she did not. If the responses are served by March 5, it would be equitable to preclude plaintiff from asserting any privilege and work product objections she may have, but nor is it equitable to allow plaintiff to assert other objections. For a simple motion, Garcia's request for $3,100 in monetary sanctions is excessive.
Order submitted for review. Visiting Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/HEK)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Aug-01-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT ALEJANDRA GARCIA's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Alejandra Garcia's motion to quash service of summons is granted. Plaintiffs Linda Harrington, et al.'s request for monetary sanctions is denied.

"When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [internal quotation marks, citation, and footnote omitted].) Plaintiffs Linda Harrington, et al. presented no evidence to carry their burden that effective service was made. Plaintiffs and Defendant agree that personal service was not executed. However, on the proof of service, section 5(a) was checked, indicating personal service. Plaintiffs' statement of facts identifies their process server as a registered process server but checked box 7(e)(1) stating that the server was not a registered California process server. The proof of service provided was overall prepared in a confusing way and contradicts Plaintiffs' statement of the facts and declaration. While Plaintiffs mention multiple times in their opposition and in Mr. Hackett's declaration a Declaration of Due Diligence which outlines the previous five attempts to personally serve Defendant, no such declaration was attached or submitted to the court. The proof of service, lack of a declaration of due diligence, and Mr. Hackett's declaration combined do not overcome the plaintiffs' burden to demonstrate that there was effective service.

Actual notice may defeat a motion to quash only when there is substantial compliance with the statute. (Ramos v. Homeward Residential, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1434, 1443-1444.) While Defendant received actual notice of the lawsuit and subsequently filed this motion to quash, there was no showing by plaintiffs of substantial compliance.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
